content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

April 28, 2008

Paul Johnston, Ph.D.
Chair, Personnel Commission
Santa Cruz City School District
245 Elk Street
Santa Cruz, CA 95065

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-08-058

Dear Dr. Johnston:

This letter is in response to your request regarding the conflict-of-interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ The Commission does not address
matters outside of the Act including those implicating Government Code Section 1090 et
seq. Because the Commission is not authorized to advise you regarding laws outside the
Act, we urge you seek advice from your agency's counsel or the Attorney General's
office on the provisions. In addition, the Commission will neither advise with respect
to an official's past conduct nor with respect to another person's duties under the Act if
the requestor of the advice has not been authorized to request advice on behalf of that
person. (Regulation 18329(b)(8)(A) and (B).) Therefore, nothing in this letter should be
construed to evaluate either any conduct that may have already taken place or the actions
of others than yourself.

QUESTIONS

1. Are members of the Personnel Commission required to file Form 700,
Statement of Economic Interests?

2. Are members of the Personnel Commission in violation of rules regarding
conflict of interest?

___________________________
¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All
statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair
Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California
Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of
Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. I-08-058
Page No. 2


CONCLUSIONS

1. As explained below, except in those instances when the Commission is the
code reviewing body, which it is not in this case, the Commission will not render advice
regarding the interpretation of an agency's conflict-of-interest code or the application of
that code to a specific individual until the person first requests a determination from the
agency and the agency's code reviewing body pursuant to Regulation 18329.5 (copy
enclosed). Therefore, you should first request a determination from the Santa Cruz Board
of Supervisors as to whether the Personnel Commission should be included in the
county's conflict-of-interest code.

2. We cannot advise you regarding conduct that has already taken place or that
relates to persons other than you. As to future conduct in which you may engage, your
letter presents insufficient information on which to base a conflict-of-interest analysis.
Specifically, we would need to know the nature of any future decisions in which you may
participate on the contract and what financial impact, if any, a decision may have on your
financial interest. Please note that the Act's conflict-of-interest rules apply to a public
official whether or not his or her position is designated in the conflict-of-interest code of
the official's agency.

FACTS

You are the Chair of the Personnel Commission for the Santa Cruz City School
District. Over the past two years, you have been advised by the personnel director who is
employed by the Personnel Commission that you need not file the Statement of
Economic Interests, Form 700, because your position is not included on the list approved
by the county office of education.

The other facts related in your letter regard the actions of others than yourself
relating to a contract made by your commission in 2006 and continuing in effect through
June 30, 2009. You provide no information regarding future decisions, if any, by your
commission concerning the contract.

File No. I-08-058
Page No. 3

ANALYSIS

In appropriate circumstances, the Act requires public officials,² to disqualify
themselves from acting in order to avoid a conflict of interest. (Section 81002(c).) To
accomplish this purpose, the Act requires that certain public officials, including members
of boards or commissions of a local government agency that are not purely advisory,
disclose financial interests in accordance with the conflict-of-interest code. This
conflict-of-interest code both enumerates the positions within the agency that make or
participate in making decisions that may have a foreseeable and material effect on the
financial interests of the official filing the position and prescribes a level of disclosure
for the official commensurate with the official's duties. Section 87301 provides that a
conflict-of-interest code should be formulated at the "most decentralized level possible,"
which means the conflict-of-interest code is developed by the official's agency. (See
Sections 87300-87313.)

Under Regulation 18329.5, the Commission will not render advice regarding the
interpretation of an agency's conflict-of-interest code or the application of that code to a
specific individual unless certain requirements are met. In those instances in which the
Commission is not the code reviewing body for the conflict-of-interest code in question,
the Commission will not render advice until the person requesting the advice, or the
person's authorized representative, first requests a determination from the agency for
which the person provides services and that agency's code reviewing body.³ (Regulation
18329.5(a)(3)(A).) Where the agency is a county agency, as it appears the Personnel

² Section 82048 states that "public official" means "every member, officer, employee, or
consultant of a state or local government agency." Regulation 18701(a), enclosed, provides that for
purposes of Section 82048 defining public official, the following definitions apply:

"(1) 'Member' shall include, but not be limited to, salaried or unsalaried members of committees,
boards or commissions with decisionsmaking authority. A committee, board or commission
possesses decision making authority whenever:

(A) It may make a final governmental decision;

(B) It may compel a governmental decision; or it may prevent a governmental decision either by
reason of an exclusive power to initiate the decision or by reason of a veto that may not be
overridden; or

(C) It makes substantive recommendations that are, and over an extended period of time have
been, regularly approved without significant amendment or modification by another public official
or governmental agency."

³ When the Commission is the code reviewing body for the conflict-of-interest code in question,
the Commission will not render advice until the person requesting the advice, or the person's authorized
representative, first requests a determination from the agency for which the person provides his or her
services. (Regulation 18329.5(a)(2).) The Commission is the code reviewing body for all state agencies,
other than an agency in the judicial branch of government, or any local government agency with
jurisdiction in more than one county. (Section 82011(a).)

File No. I-08-058
Page No. 4

Commission for the Santa Cruz City School District is, the code reviewing body is the
county's board of supervisors. (Section 82011(b).)

As the "code reviewing body" it is up to the Santa Cruz County Board of
Supervisors to make the determination regarding whether or not the members of the
Personnel Commission are subject to the county's conflict-of-interest code. Pursuant to
Regulation 18701(a), this determination will be based on whether or not the commission
has decision-making authority. It may be that, upon the county's analysis, the Personnel
Commission is found to have neither decision-making authority⁴ nor ability to compel
or prevent governmental decisions and therefore is not subject to inclusion in the
county's conflict-of-interest code and the filing of statements of economic interests.
(Regulation 18701(a)(1)(A) and (B).) Additionally, the Personnel Commission may not
have made recommendations over a sufficient period of time for the county to determine
if such recommendations are regularly approved without significant amendment or
modification by another public official or governmental agency. (Regulation
18701(a)(1)(C).)

Regarding your second question, the Commission has adopted an eight-step
standard analysis for deciding whether an official has a disqualifying conflict of interest.
(Regulation 18700.) The Act's conflict-of-interest rules apply to all public officials
regardless of whether or not their position is designated in the conflict-of-interest code of
the official's agency. The general rule is that a conflict of interest exists whenever a
public official makes, participates in making, or otherwise uses his or her official position
to influence a governmental decision that has a reasonably foreseeable material financial
effect on one or more of his or her financial interests. As stated above, the Commission
does not provide advice on conduct that has already taken place. (Regulation
18329(b)(8)(A).) Furthermore, the Commission does not provide advice regarding the
conduct of another person unless the requestor is authorized to seek the advice.
(Regulation 18329(b)(8)(B).) As to future conduct in which you may engage regarding
the contract in question, you have not presented any information with respect to any
governmental decision that you may be faced with that would implicate the conflict-of-
interest provisions of the Act. (See Section 87100, et seq; Regulation 18700,
subdivisions (b)(1) - (8) and Can I Vote? Conflicts of Interest Overview, a copy of which
is enclosed.) Because we have neither information regarding your status as a public
official nor information regarding any governmental decision you may be faced with that
would implicate the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act, we are unable to provide an
analysis of how the Act's conflict-of-interest provisions may apply to your future
decisions relating to this contract.

_______________________
⁴ Although we offer no advice, we note that it appears from the facts of your letter that the
Personnel Commission has been responsible for negotiating and entering into contracts and may be making
final governmental decisions regarding those contracts.

File No. I-08-058
Page No. 5

If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me
at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel

[signature]

By: Adrianne Korchmaros–
Political Reform Consultant
Technical Assistance Division

AK:jgl

Enclosure
